18: epistrephai apo skotous eis phos kai tes exousias tou Satana epi ton theon (epistrepsai apo skotous eis phōs kai tēs exousias tou Satana epi ton theon): Paul's mission-summary before Agrippa. Epistrephō (turn) is the LXX-standard verb for Israel's repentance/return (shub). The dual transfer — darkness to light, Satan's authority to God — frames conversion as a cosmic jurisdiction-change, not merely moral improvement. Aphesis hamartion (forgiveness of sins) and klēron en tois hēgiasmenois (inheritance among the sanctified) complete the fourfold result: light, God's side, forgiveness, inheritance. The inheritance-language echoes Deuteronomy 33:3-4 and the Wisdom literature of Second Temple Judaism.
1: Agrippos de pros ton Paulon ephē, Epitrepetai soi hyper seautou legein: 'Agrippa said to Paul: you are permitted to speak for yourself'. Epitrepetai (it is permitted) — Agrippa's formal grant of floor-time has the character of a royal indulgence rather than judicial procedure. Paul's response — ekteinAs tēn cheira (extending the hand) — is the orator's gesture (cf. Acts 13:16; Acts 21:40); Luke frames the speech as a formal rhetorical address, not an informal defense.
2: makarios egō heauton hēgēmai epi sou mellōn apologeisthai: 'I consider myself fortunate that I am going to make my defense before you'. Makarios (fortunate/blessed) — the opening compliment to Agrippa is standard rhetorical captatio benevolentiae (capturing goodwill), but Paul grounds it in Agrippa's specific competence: he is gnōstēn onta se pantōn tōn kata Ioudaious ethōn te kai zētēmatōn (an expert in all Jewish customs and disputes). Paul's case is genuinely intelligible only to someone with that background.
3: dio deomai sou makrothymōs akousai mou: 'therefore I beg you to hear me patiently'. Makrothymōs (patiently/with long-suffering) — Paul's speech will be extended and complex; he is asking for sustained attention. The word makrothymia (patience) in Paul's letters describes the divine forbearance that undergirds salvation history; here it describes the quality of attention the speech's subject matter requires.
4: ten men oun biōsin mou tēn ek neotētos tēn ap archēs genomenēn en tō ethnei mou en te Hierosolymois: Paul's autobiographical opening — his life from youth in the nation and in Jerusalem. Biōsis (way of life) is a rare term for the conduct and manner of life; Paul's biography is itself evidence for his position. The Jerusalem location is significant: Paul was educated at the center of Jewish learning, not on the diaspora periphery. What he says about Jewish hope comes from insider knowledge.
5: proginōskontes me anōthen... hoti kata tēn akribestatēn hairesin tēs hēmeteras thrēskeias ezēsa Pharisaios: 'they have known me from the beginning... that according to the strictest sect of our religion I lived as a Pharisee'. Akribestatēn (strictest/most precise) — the superlative of akribes (exact/precise); the Pharisees were known for scrupulous Torah-observance and oral tradition development. Paul's Pharisaic credentials (Philippians 3:5-6) make his resurrection-belief continuous with the best of Jewish tradition, not a departure from it.
6: kai nyn ep elpidi tēs eis tous pateras hēmōn epangelias genomenēs hypo tou theou hestēka krinomenos: 'and now I stand on trial because of the hope in the promise made by God to our fathers'. Elpidi (hope) as the legal ground of Paul's trial — he is not being tried for a crime but for a hope. The epangelia (promise) to the patriarchs is the Abrahamic promise-structure of the OT; Paul's entire mission is the announcement of its fulfillment in Christ. This is the theological center of the Agrippa speech.
7: eis hēn to dōdekaphulon hēmōn en ekteneia nychta kai hēmeran latreuon elpizei katantēsai: 'to which our twelve-tribe nation, earnestly worshipping night and day, hopes to attain'. Dōdekaphulon (twelve-tribe) — an archaic collective for all Israel, including the northern tribes scattered in the Assyrian exile. Paul's point: the resurrection-hope he proclaims is the same hope that animates Israel's whole liturgical life. The nation that prays night and day is praying toward the fulfillment Paul now announces. The charge against Paul is a charge against Israel's own prayer.
8: ti apiston krinete ei ho theos nekrous egeirei (ti apiston krinete ei ho theos nekrous egeirei): 'why is it judged incredible by you that God raises the dead?' The rhetorical question is the hinge of the argument. Apiston (unbelievable/incredible) — Paul attacks the presupposition behind the rejection. For Jews who believe in the God who created ex nihilo and parted the Red Sea, resurrection is not the most extraordinary thing imaginable. The incredibility is not epistemic but volitional — they do not want to believe what the evidence supports.
9: egō men oun edoxa emautō pros to onoma Iēsou tou Nazōraiou dein polla enantia praxai: 'I indeed supposed in myself that I had to do many things against the name of Jesus the Nazarene'. Edoxa emautō (I supposed in myself) — self-directed opinion that proved catastrophically wrong. Paul's persecution is now framed as a dei-driven misunderstanding: he thought it was necessary (dein) to oppose Christ, using the same divine-necessity vocabulary he now uses for his mission. The reversal is total.
10: ho kai epoiēsa en Hierosolymois kai pollous ton hagion egō en phylakais katekleisa: 'which I also did in Jerusalem — I locked up many of the saints in prison'. Ton hagiōn (the saints/holy ones) — Paul's term for believers is telling: the people he imprisoned were hagioi, consecrated to God. Ten para ton archiereōn exousian labōn (having received authority from the chief priests): the institutional backing for his persecution was official religious authority — he was the establishment's instrument.
11: pollakins timōrōn autous ēnankazOn blasphēmein: 'punishing them often in all the synagogues, I was trying to force them to blaspheme'. Ēnankazon (I was trying to compel/force) — imperfect tense, repeated attempted coercion. Blasphēmein — the forced blasphemy would be renouncing Jesus as Lord; Paul was trying to break faith under duress. Perissōs te emmainomenos autois (being enraged/madly furious against them): emmainomai (to be mad/enraged) is a strong term for irrational, obsessive fury — Luke portrays the pre-Damascus Paul as religious mania.
12: en hois poreuo menos eis tēn Damaskon met exousias kai epitropēs tēs ton archiereōn: the Damascus journey as the culmination of official persecution. Met exousias kai epitropēs (with authority and commission) — double credentialing for maximum legal force. The Damascus mission was not freelance aggression but institutional policy, which makes the reversal on the road all the more total: the official agent of suppression became the most effective apostle.
13: hēmeras mesēs kata tēn hodon eidon... phōs hyper tēn lampotēta tou hēliou: 'at midday on the road I saw... a light brighter than the sun'. Hēmeras mesēs (midday) — the brightest time of day, emphasizing the supernatural intensity. Hyper tēn lampotēta tou hēliou (beyond the brightness of the sun) — this detail only in the third telling. The three Damascus accounts (Acts 9, 22, 26) progressively intensify the theophanic elements; the third, before the royal audience, is the most explicitly visionary.
14: sklēron soi pros kentra laktizein (sklēron soi pros kentra laktizein): 'it is hard for you to kick against the goads'. A Greek proverbial expression (found in Pindar, Aeschylus, Euripides) for futile resistance to a superior power. Its use here (only in this third telling) suggests the risen Christ is using culturally intelligible language with Paul the Hellenistic Jew. Kentra (goads) are the sharp sticks used to drive oxen; the animal that kicks back only injures itself more. Paul's persecution was already futile resistance to a divine compulsion he couldn't yet recognize.
15: egō de eipon, Tis ei kyrie? Ho de kyrios eipen, Egō eimi Iēsous hon sy diōkeis: 'I said: who are you, lord? And the Lord said: I am Jesus whom you are persecuting'. The three Damascus accounts share this core exchange verbatim. Egō eimi (I am) echoes the divine self-disclosure formula; in John, Jesus uses it absolutely of his divine identity. The identification — Jesus with the persecuted church — is the theological payoff: Paul was persecuting God's own Son without knowing it.
16: all anastēthi kai stēthi epi tous podas sou... procheirisasthai se hyperetēn kai martyra: 'but rise and stand on your feet... I have appeared to you for this: to appoint you a servant and witness'. Procheirizō (appoint/choose beforehand) — the divine appointment precedes the mission; Paul is not a self-appointed apostle but a commissioned one. Hyperetēn kai martyra (servant and witness) — the two-term commission defines Paul's identity. Hyperetēs (under-rower, subordinate assistant) is deliberately humble; Paul is placed in service below the one who commissions him.
17: exairoumenos se ek tou laou kai ek tōn ethnōn eis ous egō apostello se: 'delivering you from the people and from the Gentiles to whom I am sending you'. Exaireō (deliver/rescue) — the rescue language frames Paul's entire ministry as sustained divine protection from the threats the trial-narrative has just documented. Eis ous egō apostello se (to whom I am sending you) — the apostolic apostellō (send) is from the risen Christ himself; Paul's authority derives from this direct commission.
19: hothen, basileu Agrippe, ouk egenomēn apeithēs tē ouranīō optasia: 'therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision'. Apeithēs (disobedient) — Paul's summary of his post-Damascus response is framed as obedience. Ouranīō optasia (heavenly vision) — the Damascus road as visionary revelation, not just physical event. Paul's entire missionary life is a continuous act of obedience to a single command received on one day: the logic of the speech leads from vision to obedience to trial.
20: alla tois en Damaskon prōton te kai Hierosolymois kai pasan tēn chōran tēs Ioudaias kai tois ethnesin apēggellon metanoein kai epistrephein epi ton theon: the geographic summary of Paul's mission matches the Acts narrative — Damascus (Acts 9:20-22), Jerusalem (Acts 9:28-29), Judea, and the Gentiles (the three journeys). Metanoein kai epistrephein epi ton theon (repent and turn to God) — the same two-movement gospel Paul proclaimed at Lystra (Acts 14:15) and Thessalonica. The content is consistent across all audiences.
21: heneka toutōn me Ioudaioi syllabenomenos en tō hierō epeirōnto diacheirisasthai: 'on account of these things the Jews seized me in the temple and were trying to kill me'. Diacheirizō (take in hand, kill) — the attempt to kill Paul in the temple (Acts 21:30-32) is what his obedience to the Damascus commission provoked. The sentence completes the logic: vision → obedience → mission → arrest. Paul is on trial for doing exactly what the risen Christ commanded him.
22: epikourias oun tychōn tēs apo tou theou achri tēs hēmeras tautēs hestēka martyromenos: 'having obtained help from God, I have stood to this day witnessing'. Epikourias tychōn (having obtained help) — epikouría is the word for divine or royal aid in a crisis. Paul's two years in prison have been sustained by the same God who commissioned him; his continued standing (hestēka) is itself evidence of the commission's validity. Martyromenos (witnessing) — Paul is witnessing even now, before this court, fulfilling the Damascus commission in the act of his defense.
23: ei pathētos ho Christos, ei prōtos ex anastaseōs nekrōn phōs mellei kataggellein tō te laō kai tois ethnesin: 'that the Christ would suffer, that first from the resurrection of the dead he would proclaim light to the people and to the Gentiles'. The two-part kerygmatic thesis: (1) the Messiah was to suffer (pathētos — morally possible/destined to suffer); (2) as the first-fruits of resurrection, he would bring light to both Jews and Gentiles. Prōtos ex anastaseōs (first from resurrection) — Christ as the pioneer-resurrection who opens the category for all who follow (1 Corinthians 15:20-23).
24: ho Phēstos megalē tē phonē ephē, Mainē Paule: ta polla se grammata eis manian peritrepei: 'Festus said with a loud voice: you are out of your mind, Paul — your great learning is turning you to madness'. Mainē (you are mad) — Festus's interruption at the resurrection claim is the educated Roman administrator's response to what sounds like metaphysical fantasy. Grammata (letters/learning) — Festus attributes Paul's views to scholarly overreach. The charge is the mirror-image of the Corinthians' objection (1 Corinthians 1:20-23): the wisdom of this age finds resurrection 'madness'.
25: ou mainomai, phēsin, kratiste Phēste, alla alētheias kai sōphrosynēs rhēmata apophthengomai: 'I am not mad, most excellent Festus, but I utter words of truth and soundness of mind'. Sōphrosynēs (soundness of mind/sobriety) — the opposite of mania; Paul claims his is the reasoning that corresponds to reality. Apophthengomai (utter/declare) is the inspired-speech term Luke uses for the Pentecost proclamation (Acts 2:4, 14). Paul's response to the madness charge is: what I say is both true and rational — the failure is Festus's inability to process the evidence for resurrection.
26: epistamai gar autos ho basileus peri toutōn pros hon kai parrēsiasamenos lalō: 'for the king knows about these things, to whom I also speak freely'. Parrēsiasamenos (speaking freely/boldly) — parrēsia is the boldness of open, fearless speech; Paul speaks to Agrippa without diplomatic evasion because Agrippa has the Jewish background to evaluate the claims. Lanthanein auton ti toutōn ou peithomai (I am not persuaded that any of these things is hidden from him): Paul appeals to Agrippa's tacit knowledge — the events of Jesus's ministry were not done in a corner (en gōnia, Acts 26:26).
27: pisteueis basileu Agrippe tois prophētais? oida hoti pisteueis: 'do you believe the prophets, King Agrippa? I know that you believe'. Paul's direct challenge puts Agrippa in a bind: he cannot publicly deny the prophets without losing his standing with his Jewish subjects; if he affirms them, Paul's argument forces him toward acknowledging Christ. The rhetorical trap is elegant: Paul answers for Agrippa before the king can answer — 'I know that you believe' — removing the escape route of claimed ignorance.
28: ho de Agrippos pros ton Paulon, En oligō me peitheis Christianon poiēsai: 'Agrippa said to Paul: in a little you persuade me to make a Christian'. En oligō (in a little/briefly) — the phrase is ambiguous: 'in a short time', 'with little effort', or 'almost'. It is likely ironic — Agrippa deflects Paul's challenge with urbane humor rather than genuine engagement. Christianon — the third and only NT occurrence in Acts of the word 'Christian' (Acts 11:26; Acts 26:28; 1 Peter 4:16); Agrippa uses it as an outsider term, which confirms its origin as a non-Christian designation.
29: ho de Paulos, Euxaimēn an tō theō kai en oligō kai en megalō ou monon se alla kai pantas tous akouontas mou sēmeron genesthai toioutous hopoios kai egō eimi parektos tōn desmōn toutōn: Paul's closing wish — that all present would become what he is, except for the chains. Euxaimēn (I would pray/wish) — optative of prayer. The inclusion of parektos tōn desmōn toutōn (except for these chains) is both self-deprecating humor and a pointed comparison: Paul is a prisoner speaking to the free, and he wishes them the freedom he has found.
30: anestē te ho basileus kai ho hēgemōn hē te Bernikē kai hoi synkathēmenoi autois: the formal dissolution of the audience — all stand, indicating the hearing is complete. The joint standing of king, governor, and Bernice is a mark of protocol. The subsequent private discussion (Acts 26:31) reflects genuine deliberation: Agrippa, Bernice, and Festus confer away from the formal setting.
31: kai anachōrēsantes elaloun pros allēlous legontes hoti Ouden thanatou axion e desmōn prAssei ho anthrōpos houtos: 'and having withdrawn they talked to one another saying: this man is doing nothing worthy of death or imprisonment'. Axios thanatou ē desmōn (worthy of death or bonds) — the standard Roman categories for capital and non-capital offenses. This is the fifth and clearest acquittal in the trial sequence; Agrippa and Festus reach the same conclusion that Lysias, Felix, and Festus had individually reached. The judicial verdict is unanimous: Paul is innocent.
32: Agrippos de tō Phēstō ephē, Apolelysthai edynato ho anthrōpos houtos ei mē epekeklēto Kaisara: 'Agrippa said to Festus: this man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar'. The conclusion of Acts' Roman judicial sequence: Paul is innocent by every court that has examined him; his appeal to Caesar is the one procedural step that makes release impossible. Luke's irony is precise — the appeal that Paul made for protection is also the mechanism that ensures he reaches Rome, fulfilling the Lord's promise of 23:11 by the most indirect possible route.
